html:
II

The crossing was over. They were arriving. The movement of the little steamer that had collected the passengers from the packet boat drove the raw air against MiriamвАЩs face. In her tired brain the grey river and the flat misty shores slid constantly into a vision of the gaslit dining room at homeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the large clear glowing fire, the sounds of the family voices. Every effort to obliterate the picture brought back again the moment that had come at the dinner table as they all sat silent for an instant with downcast eyes and she had suddenly longed to go on forever just sitting there with them all.

Now, in the boat she wanted to be free for the strange grey river and the grey shores. But the home scenes recurred relentlessly. Again and again she went through the last momentsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the goodbyes, the unexpected convulsive force of her motherвАЩs arms, her own dreadful inability to give any answering embrace. She could not remember saying a single word. There had been a feeling that came like a tide carrying her away. Eager and dumb and remorseful she had gone out of the house and into the cab with Sarah, and then had come the long sitting in the loop-line trainвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬtalk about somethingвАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Sarah sitting opposite and her unchanged voice saying вАЬWhat shall we talk about?вАЭ And then a long waiting, and the brown leather strap swinging against the yellow grained door, the smell of dust and the dirty wooden flooring, with the noise of the wheels underneath going to the swinging tune of one of HellerвАЩs Sleepless Nights. The train had made her sway with its movements. How still Sarah seemed to sit, fixed in the old life. Nothing had come but strange cruel emotions.

After the suburban train nothing was distinct until the warm snowflakes were drifting against her face through the cold darkness on Harwich quay. Then, after what seemed like a great loop of time spent going helplessly up a gangway towards вАЬthe worldвАЭ she had stood, face to face with the pale polite stewardess in her cabin. вАЬI had better have a lemon, cut in two,вАЭ she had said, feeling suddenly stifled with fear. For hours she had lain despairing, watching the slowly swaying walls of her cabin or sinking with closed eyes through invertebrate dipping spaces. Before each releasing paroxysm she told herself вАЬthis is like death; one day I shall die, it will be like this.вАЭ

She supposed there would be breakfast soon on shore, a firm room and a teapot and cups and saucers. Cold and exhaustion would come to an end. She would be talking to her father.

He was standing near her with the Dutchman who had helped her off the boat and looked after her luggage. The Dutchman was listening, deferentially. Miriam saw the strong dark blue beam of his eyes.

вАЬVery good, very good,вАЭ she heard him say, вАЬfine education in German schools.вАЭ

Both men were smoking cigars.

She wanted to draw herself upright and shake out her clothes.

вАЬSelect,вАЭ she heard, вАЬexcellent staff of mastersвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ daughters of gentlemen.вАЭ

вАЬPater is trying to make the Dutchman think I am being taken as a pupil to a finishing school in Germany.вАЭ She thought of her lonely pilgrimage to the West End agency, of her humiliating interview, of her heart-sinking acceptance of the post, the excitements and misgivings she had had, of her sudden challenge of them all that evening after dinner, and their dismay and remonstrance and reproachesвБ†вАФof her fear and determination in insisting and carrying her point and making them begin to be interested in her plan.

But she shared her fatherвАЩs satisfaction in impressing the Dutchman. She knew that she was at one with him in that. She glanced at him. There could be no doubt that he was playing the role of the English gentleman. Poor dear. It was what he had always wanted to be. He had sacrificed everything to the idea of being a вАЬperson of leisure and cultivation.вАЭ Well, after all, it was true in a way. He wasвБ†вАФand he had, she knew, always wanted her to be the same and she was going to finish her education abroadвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ in Germany.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They were nearing a little low quay backed by a tremendous saffron-coloured hoarding announcing in black letters вАЬSunlight Zeep.вАЭ

вАЬDid you see, Pater; did you see?вАЭ

They were walking rapidly along the quay.

вАЬDid you see? Sunlight Zeep!вАЭ

She listened to his slightly scuffling stride at her side.

Glancing up she saw his face excited and important. He was not listening. He was being an English gentleman, вАЬemergingвАЭ from the Dutch railway station.

вАЬSunlight Zeep,вАЭ she shouted. вАЬZeep, Pater!вАЭ

He glanced down at her and smiled condescendingly.

вАЬAh, yes,вАЭ he admitted with a laugh.

There were Dutch faces for MiriamвБ†вАФmen, women and children coming towards her with sturdy gait.

вАЬTheyвАЩre talking Dutch! TheyвАЩre all talking Dutch!вАЭ

The foreign voices, the echoes in the little narrow street, the flat waterside effect of the sounds, the bright clearness she had read of, brought tears to her eyes.

вАЬThe others must come here,вАЭ she told herself, pitying them all.

They had an English breakfast at the Victoria Hotel and went out and hurried about the little streets. They bought cigars and rode through the town on a little tramway. Presently they were in a train watching the Dutch landscape go by. One level stretch succeeded another. Miriam wanted to go out alone under the grey sky and walk over the flat fields shut in by poplars.

She looked at the dykes and the windmills with indifferent eyes, but her desire for the flat meadows grew.

Late at night, seated wide-awake opposite her sleeping companion, rushing towards the German city, she began to think.

It was a foolвАЩs errand.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ To undertake to go to the German school and teachвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ to be going thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ with nothing to give. The moment would come when there would be a class sitting round a table waiting for her to speak. She imagined one of the rooms at the old school, full of scornful girls.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How was English taught? How did you begin? English grammarвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ in German? Her heart beat in her throat. She had never thought of thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the rules of English grammar? Parsing and analysis.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Anglo-Saxon prefixes and suffixesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ gerundial infinitive.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was too late to look anything up. Perhaps there would be a class tomorrow.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The German lessons at school had been dreadfully good.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fr√§uleinвАЩs grave faceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ her perfect knowledge of every ruleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ her clear explanations in EnglishвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ her examples.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ All these things were there, in English grammar.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And she had undertaken to teach them and could not even speak German.

MonsieurвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ had talked French all the timeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ dict√©esвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ lecturesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Le ConscritвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ WaterlooвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬLa Maison d√©serteвАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ his careful voice reading on and onвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ until the room disappeared.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She must do that for her German girls. Read English to them and make them happy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But first there must be verbsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ there had been cahiers of themвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ first, second, third conjugation.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was impudence, an impudent invasionвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the dreadful clever, foreign school.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They would laugh at her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She began to repeat the English alphabet.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She doubted whether, faced with a class, she could reach the end without a mistake.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She reached z and went on to the parts of speech.

There would be a moment when she must have an explanation with the Fr√§ulein. Perhaps she could tell her that she found the teaching was beyond her scope and then find a place somewhere as a servant. She remembered things she had heard about German servantsвБ†вАФthat whenever they even dusted a room they cleaned the windows and on Sundays they waited at lunch in muslin dresses and afterwards went to balls. She feared even the German servants would despise her. They had never been allowed into the kitchen at home except when there was jam-makingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she had never made a bed in her life.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A shop? But that would mean knowing German and being quick at giving change. Impossible. Perhaps she could find some English people in Hanover who would help her. There was an English colony she knew, and an English church. But that would be like going back. That must not happen. She would rather stay abroad on any termsвБ†вАФaway from EnglandвБ†вАФEnglish people. She had scented something, a sort of confidence, everywhere, in her hours in Holland, the brisk manner of the German railway officials and the serene assurance of the travelling Germans she had seen, confirmed her impression. Away out here, the sense of imminent catastrophe that had shadowed all her life so far, had disappeared. Even here in this dim carriage, with disgrace ahead she felt that there was freedom somewhere at hand. Whatever happened she would hold to that.

She glanced up at her small leather handbag lying in the rack and thought of the solid money in her purse. Twenty-five shillings. It was a large sum and she was to have more as she needed.

She glanced across at the pale face with its point of reddish beard, the long white hands laid one upon the other on the crossed knees. He had given her twenty-five shillings and there was her fare and his, and his return fare and her new trunk and all the things she had needed. It must be the end of taking money from him. She was grown up. She was the strong-minded one. She must manage. With a false position ahead and after a short space, disaster, she must get along.

The peaceful Dutch fields came to her mind. They looked so secure. They had passed by too soon. We have always been in a false position, she pondered. Always lying and pretending and keeping up a showвБ†вАФnever daring to tell anybody.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Did she want to tell anybody? To come out into the open and be helped and have things arranged for her and do things like other people? No.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬMiriam always likes to be differentвАЭвБ†вАФвАЬSociety is no boon to those not sociable.вАЭ Dreadful thingsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and the girls laughing together about them. What did they really mean?

вАЬSociety is no boon to those not sociableвАЭвБ†вАФon her birthday page in Ellen SharpeвАЩs birthday book. Ellen handed it to her going upstairs and had chanted the words out to the others and smiled her smileвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she had not asked her to write her nameвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ was it unsociable to dislike so many of the girls.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ EllenвАЩs people were in the IndianвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ her thoughts hesitated.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ SivvleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ something grandвБ†вАФAll the grand girls were horridвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ somehow mean and slyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ SivvleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ SivvleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Civil! Of course! Civil what?

Miriam groaned. She was a governess now. Someone would ask her that question. She would ask Pater before he went.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, she would not.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If only he would answer a question simply, and not with a superior air as if he had invented the thing he was telling about. She felt she had a right to all the knowledge there was, without fussвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ oh, without fussвБ†вАФwithout fuss andвБ†вАФemotion.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I am unsociable, I supposeвБ†вАФshe mused. She could not think of anyone who did not offend her. I donвАЩt like men and I loathe women. I am a misanthrope. SoвАЩs Pater. He despises women and canвАЩt get on with men. We are differentвБ†вАФitвАЩs us, him and me. HeвАЩs failed us because heвАЩs different and if he werenвАЩt we should be like other people. Everything in the railway responded and agreed. Like other peopleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ horrible.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She thought of the fathers of girls she knewвБ†вАФthe Poole girls, for instance, they were to be вАЬindependentвАЭ trained and certificatedвБ†вАФshe envied thatвБ†вАФbut her envy vanished when she remembered how heartily she had agreed when Sarah called them вАЬsharpвАЭ and вАЬknowing.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Poole was a business manвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ commonвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ trade.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If Pater had kept to GrandpaвАЩs business they would be trade, tooвБ†вАФwell-off, nowвБ†вАФall married. Perhaps as it was he had thought they would marry.

She thought sleepily of her Wesleyan grandparents, gravely reading the Wesleyan Methodist Recorder, the shop at Babington, her fatherвАЩs discontent, his solitary fishing and reading, his discovery of musicвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ scienceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ classical music in the first Novello editionsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ FaradayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ speaking to Faraday after lectures. MarriageвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the new houseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the red brick wall at the end of the garden where young peach trees were plantedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ running up and downstairs and singingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ both of them singing in the rooms and the gardenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she sometimes with her hair down and then when visitors were expected pinned in coils under a little cap and wearing a small hoopвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the garden and lawns and shrubbery and the long kitchen garden and the summerhouse under the oaks beyond and the pretty old gabled вАЬtownвАЭ on the river and the woods all along the river valley and the hills shining up out of the mist. The snow man they both made in the winterвБ†вАФthe birth of Sarah and then EveвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ his studies and book-buyingвБ†вАФand after five years her own disappointing birth as the third girl, and the coming of Harriett just over a year laterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ her motherвАЩs illness, money troublesвБ†вАФtheir two years at the sea to retrieveвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the disappearance of the sunlit red-walled garden always in full summer sunshine with the sound of bees in it or dark from windowsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the narrowing of the house life down to the Marine VillaвБ†вАФwith the sea creeping inвБ†вАФwading out through the green shallows, out and out till you were more than waist-deepвБ†вАФshrimping and prawning hour after hour for weeks togetherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ poking in the rock pools, watching the sun and the colours in the strange afternoonsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ then the sudden large house at Barnes with the вАЬdriveвАЭ winding to the door.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He used to come home from the City and the Constitutional Club and sometimes instead of reading The Times or the Globe or the Proceedings of the British Association or Herbert Spencer, play Pope Joan or Jacoby with them all, or Table Billiards and laugh and be вАЬsillyвАЭ and take his turn at being вАЬbumpedвАЭ by Timmy going the round of the long dining room table, tail in the air; he had taken Sarah and Eve to see Don Giovanni and WinterвАЩs Tale and the new piece, Lohengrin. No one at the tennis club had seen that. He had good taste. No one else had been to Madame SchumannвАЩs FarewellвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ sitting at the piano with her curtains of hair and her dreamy smileвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and the Philharmonic Concerts. No one else knew about the lectures at the Royal Institution, beginning at nine on Fridays.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No one elseвАЩs father went with a party of scientific men вАЬfor the advancement of scienceвАЭ to Norway or America, seeing the Falls and the Yosemite Valley. No one else took his children as far as Dawlish for the holidays, travelling all day, from eight until sevenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no esplanade, the old stone jetty and coves and cowrie shells.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶
